``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined Vermont judges have discretion to vary the relationship between the minimum and maximum prison terms they impose. In most cases both the judicial minimum and maximum are subject to potential reductions through credit earnings. Starting in 2021 earned time credits of 7 days per month reduce both minimum and maximum. Serious “listed” offenses are subject to traditional discretionary parole while unlisted offenses are subject to presumptive more automatic release.

# Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 38%, changing from 2,079 in 2010 to 1,287 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Source: BJS Prisoners Series

``` ::: ::: Source: BJS Prisoners 2022 - Race and Ethnicity

# Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2017 to 2020, prison releases decreased 48%, dropping from 3,913 in 2017 to 2,018 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2016, 0% of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 0% increase compared to 2010. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics of People Released The demographics of people released from prison reveal notable proportions among White, non-Hispanic and black, non-hispanic people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old. These findings provide insights into the populations transitioning back into the community. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Time Served by Offense Between 2017 and 2020, there were shifts in the average time served by individuals for different offense types in Vermont. The largest change was for property offenses, which increased by 89%. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
# Disparities ## Incarceration Rates In Vermont, Black, non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 8.6 times higher than White, non-Hispanic people, when accounting for population sizes in the community. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Years Spent in Prison After Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender :::
## Timing of Release After Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity The timing of release for individuals after their parole eligibility year varied by offense type and demographic group, with many remaining in prison for several years beyond their eligibility date. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender :::
## Time Served ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: White, non-Hispanic individuals faced the longest average time served in prison in 2020, with an average of 0.6 years. Other race(s), non-Hispanic individuals experienced shorter prison stays, averaging 0.4 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: By offense type, disparities were observed in time served by race and ethnicity. For Rape or Sexual Assault offenses, Black, non-Hispanic individuals had 1.67 more years on average compared to Hispanic, any race individuals, who had the shortest time served for these offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender :::
